claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardizes how AI applications interact with external systems through three primary interfaces: prompts, tools, and resources, replacing the fragmented N×M problem of custom integrations
actionable: When building AI applications, use MCP servers for consistent integration patterns instead of building custom hooks for each data source or tool
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Tools in MCP are model-controlled: the LLM decides when to invoke them. Resources are application-controlled: the client decides when to use them. Prompts are user-controlled: users invoke them as slash commands or templates
actionable: Design MCP servers with clear separation: tools for LLM decision-making, resources for application logic, prompts for user-initiated interactions
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Sampling allows MCP servers to request LLM completions from clients rather than hosting their own models, enabling federated intelligence where the client owns all LLM interactions and can enforce privacy/cost controls
actionable: Use sampling in MCP servers that need intelligence but shouldn't host LLMs themselves; the client can set rate limits, choose models, and enforce privacy policies
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Composability in MCP is a logical separation, not physical: any application can be both a client AND a server, enabling multi-layer agent architectures where agents can chain and call other agents
actionable: Build hierarchical agent systems where top-level orchestrator agents invoke sub-agent servers, each specializing in specific tasks, all coordinated through MCP
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Modern agent architectures follow the augmented LLM pattern: LLM + retrieval systems + tools + memory/state running in a loop. MCP becomes the standardized bottom layer providing all external interactions
actionable: When designing agents, focus on the core loop (task → tools → response → repeat) and use MCP servers to expose all external capabilities rather than building integrations directly
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: OAuth 2.0 support in MCP allows servers to handle authentication securely: servers orchestrate the auth flow, hold tokens, and federate access to users via session tokens, removing DevX friction of local auth
actionable: Implement OAuth 2.0 in MCP servers for remote APIs; let the server handle token management so clients don't need to store credentials
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Remote MCP servers over SSE (Server-Sent Events) remove the need for local installation and standard IO, enabling any URL-hosted server to be discovered and used without users understanding MCP internals
actionable: Host MCP servers on public URLs using SSE transport for maximum accessibility; users can connect without local setup
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: The mcp_agent framework (by Last Mile AI) simplifies agent orchestration: define sub-agents with access to specific MCP servers, and the framework handles planning, looping, and agent coordination
actionable: Use the mcp_agent Python framework to build multi-agent systems: declare agent roles, give them server access, and the framework handles orchestration
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Building MCP servers is quick and accessible: Anthropic staff built 15 official servers in ~45 minutes each using Claude, with most implementations ~200-300 lines of code focused on exposing APIs
actionable: Use MCP as your first choice for exposing APIs to LLMs; server implementation is straightforward and can be auto-generated for simple wrappers
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: An MCP registry (in development) will centralize server discovery and verification, solving the current fragmentation problem where 1100+ community servers exist with no unified discovery mechanism
actionable: When building production agents, await the official MCP registry for verified, discoverable server access rather than manually configuring multiple sources
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Self-evolving agents can discover and dynamically install new capabilities via the MCP registry at runtime without pre-programming, enabling agents to expand their toolset as needed
actionable: Design agents with the ability to search the MCP registry and install new servers on-demand; empower agents to self-improve by discovering new data sources and tools
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Claude is particularly well-suited for tool use and agentic workflows due to its training, not due to anything inherent to MCP itself; MCP is protocol-agnostic and works with any LLM
actionable: Use MCP with any LLM provider; Claude's advantage is in tool use capability, not in MCP compatibility
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: The .well-known/mcp.json pattern allows companies to advertise their official MCP endpoints, complementing the registry for top-down discovery and verification of enterprise APIs
actionable: Publish .well-known/mcp.json on your domain to let agents discover your official MCP endpoints; use with OAuth 2.0 for secure access
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Computer use (visual agent) + MCP can coexist in one agent: use MCP for APIs that are exposed, fall back to computer use for UIs without APIs, creating a hybrid approach
actionable: Build agents that first try MCP APIs for efficiency, then use computer use for systems without API coverage
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Claude for Desktop and IDEs like Cursor/Windsurf are MCP clients that allow users to attach resources and invoke tools; servers don't need custom UI logic
actionable: Build MCP servers assuming Claude for Desktop or IDE integration; don't build custom UI for tool invocation
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: MCP complements but doesn't replace agent frameworks: MCP handles context/tools, frameworks handle knowledge management and agentic loops; they work together
actionable: Use MCP servers with your existing agent framework (LangGraph, etc.); use adapters to connect frameworks to MCP
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Tool call limits for LLMs: Claude handles up to ~200 tools effectively, beyond that use tool search/RAG to filter relevant tools without overwhelming context
actionable: If exposing 50+ tools to an LLM, implement a tool-search mechanism (RAG or fuzzy search) to filter dynamically; don't prompt with all tools at once
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Resource notifications are a protocol capability (not yet widely used): servers can notify clients when resources change, enabling real-time updates without polling
actionable: Implement server-initiated resource notifications for real-time updates; clients can subscribe and receive push notifications when server state changes
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Dynamic resources and prompts in MCP can be interpolated with user/application context, not just static files: the server customizes responses based on incoming data
actionable: Build resources and prompts that accept context from the client and return customized data; avoid static-only implementations
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic

claim: Klein IDE (Zed) has an MCP auto-generator tool that can generate simple MCP servers on-the-fly, reducing barrier to adoption for basic API wrappers
actionable: Use IDE auto-generators to quickly scaffold simple MCP servers for basic API integrations
source_title: Building Agents with Model Context Protocol - Full Workshop with Mahesh Murag of Anthropic
